issues.description: The fourth selection question reads 'quadratic in the denominator with no real roots? Complete the square -- that's almost always an arctan.' A reasonable student will over-generalize 'complete the square => arctan' as a rule. Completing the square on an irreducible quadratic denominator only yields a pure arctan when the NUMERATOR is constant (or reduces to one). If the numerator contains an x-term (e.g. integral of (2x+3)/(x^2+2x+5)), the same completed-square denominator produces a ln term plus an arctan term, not 'almost always an arctan.' The hedge 'almost always' softens but does not correct the mental model, and the only worked example (17) conveniently has a constant numerator, reinforcing the false rule.
issues.fix: Add a clause: 'complete the square -- if the top is just a constant, that's an arctan; if the top still has an x, split it into a log piece plus an arctan piece first.' At minimum drop the over-broad 'almost always an arctan' framing.

issues.description: States flatly 'For definite ones, leave it off -- it's just noise.' This is operationally fine but the absolute phrasing can seed the inverse misconception that +C is wrong/forbidden on definite integrals. The reason it cancels (F(b)+C)-(F(a)+C) is given, which is good, but pairing 'never on definite' (echoed in 19_recap) with 'it's just noise' nudges a literal-minded student toward thinking writing +C in a definite computation is an error rather than merely redundant. Borderline; does not produce a calculation-level false belief.
issues.fix: Soften to 'you don't need +C for a definite integral -- it always cancels' rather than the stronger 'never' / 'noise' phrasing in 08 and 19.

issues.description: The 1/x case is stated as 'the natural log of the absolute value of x, plus C' which correctly includes the absolute value -- good. However the surrounding intuition 'Bump the exponent up, divide by the new exponent' is presented as the reverse power rule and then the n=-1 case is carved out as 'the exception.' A learner can still mistakenly apply the bump-and-divide template to 1/x (n=-1) by reflex because the exception is mentioned only after the procedural slogan; the script never explicitly says WHY (you'd divide by n+1 = 0). Not a false statement, just a missed inoculation against the most common reverse-power-rule error.
issues.fix: Add one clause explaining the exception mechanically: 'n=-1 is excluded because n+1 would be zero and you cannot divide by zero -- that case becomes ln|x| instead.'

issues.description: The 'play the movie backward' / 'leftover ghost of a chain rule' framing is vivid but states the chain-rule footprint as if the inside-derivative is ALWAYS literally present as a clean factor. A student primed by this hook (and 09_usub_motivation 'a leftover factor that looks like its derivative') may believe u-sub only works when du appears exactly, and give up when it appears off by a constant -- yet section 12 immediately requires the off-by-a-constant fix. The hook slightly oversells the 'exact ghost' mental model relative to what the lesson actually does.
issues.fix: Foreshadow the constant-fix in the hook or motivation: '...a leftover factor that looks like its derivative, maybe off by a constant we can patch up.'

notes: Arithmetic and the absolute-value/+C/bound-change mechanics are all correct and internally consistent; the only real risk is the over-broad 'complete the square => arctan' generalization in 18, which can induce a wrong rule for non-constant numerators.
